rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF VISHWAS A. NARURKAR
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Or Modify Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records Directed To Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. (Added For The Purpose Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] Having asserted that decedent's financial records tend to show decedent's true testamentary intent, Contestants now seek to limit or bar Petitioner's access to these same records. The subpoenaed information is relevant to this proceeding or is likely to lead to the discovery of relevant information. (See Evid. Code sections 1000, 1019.) Contestants have failed to carry their burden under Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.1(a) in that they have not shown grounds justifying the quashing of the subpoena, nor have they shown that allowing the production will expose them to "unreasonable or oppressive demands, including unreasonable violations of their right of privacy of the person." (CCP section 1987.1(a).) Contestants are incorrect that the propounding party has the initial burden in the context of privilege claims brought against a discovery request. The party claiming a privilege in response to a discovery request has the initial burden of proving the preliminary facts to show the privilege applies. (Lopez v. Watchtower Bible & Tract Soc'y of New York, Inc. (2016) 246 CA4th 566, 596, citing Roman Catholic Archbishop of Los Angeles v. Superior Court (2005) 131 Cal.App.4th 417, 442.) The party claiming the privilege generally makes the required preliminary showing by declarations. (Citizens for Ceres v Sup. Ct (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 889, 911.) Contestants have not met this initial burden, and the burden has not shifted to Petitioner to show a particularized need for the confidential information sought. (Britt v. Sup.Ct. (San Diego Unified Port Dist.) (1978) 20 Cal.3d 844, 859-862). Under the third Hill element, Petitioner has demonstrated a "need to know" the contents of the specified financial records in the context of this case. Petitioner's legitimate interest in obtaining the records outweighs the dubious privacy interest Contestants claim to have in decedent's financial information. Moreover, that privacy interest, if it existed, has been diminished by Contestants via their affirmative use of this financial information in asserting their own claims in challenging the Will's validity. Put plainly, the need for disclosure outweighs Contestants' privacy interests. Petitioner shall prepare and lodge with the court clerk in Department 204 a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling prior to the hearing. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on July 14, 2021. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925 and passcode 722809. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 87580177925#, participant ID 0#, and passcode 722809#. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 2 of 2]

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF VISHWAS A. NARURKAR
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Or Modify Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records Directed To Wells Fargo Bank, N.A.; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Alberto J. Campain In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Contestants' Motion to Quash or Modify Subpoena for Production of Business Records Directed to Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.1, is DENIED. The test to determine whether the subpoena duces tecum for decedent's records held by Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. violates Contestants' privacy interest is the three-prong test articulated in Hill v. Nat'l Collegiate Athletic Assn. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 1, 35. The California Supreme Court "decline[d] to hold that every assertion of a privacy interest [] must be overcome by a 'compelling interest.'" The Court then described and applied the "elements of the cause of action for violation of the state constitutional right to privacy and the defenses that might be asserted against such a cause of action." The elements are: (1) a legally protected privacy interest; (2) a reasonable expectation of privacy in the circumstances; and (3) conduct by defendant constituting a serious invasion of privacy, and a final balance of the privacy interest against "other interests." (Id. at 34-40.) The Court also distinguished between kinds of privacy interests and the legally separate assessments thereof. "Just as the right to privacy is not absolute, privacy interests do not encompass all conceivable assertions of individual rights. Legally recognized privacy interests are generally of two classes: (1) interests in precluding the dissemination or misuse of sensitive and confidential information ("informational privacy"); and (2) interests in making intimate personal decisions or conducting personal activities without observation, intrusion, or interference ("autonomy privacy"). (Id. at 35.) The privacy interest at issue here falls into the category of "informational privacy." Under the first element of the Hill test, decedent himself would certainly have had a privacy interest in his financial records (see Cal. Const., art. I section 1). However, there is no showing in the papers that Contestants have a privacy interest in decedent's financial records. The parties appear to agree that the records sought by the instant subpoena duces tecum are Wells Fargo Bank accounts allegedly held only in decedent's name, which allegedly list decedent's mother, Leela Narurkar, as the Payable on Death account beneficiary. (See Petitioner's Opposition to this motion at 2:19-24 and 4:14-15, and Contestants' Reply to the Opposition at 2:11-12; Contestants' Opposition to MSJ/MSA, MPA at 5:11-18.) Under the second Hill element, whatever reasonable expectation of privacy Contestants might have asserted over decedent's informational privacy is substantially reduced because Contestants have put the information contained in these accounts at issue in this litigation. (See Heller v. Norcal Mut. Ins. Co. (1994) 8 Cal. 4th 30, 43.) Contestants have argued in this proceeding that decedent's expressions of testamentary intent regarding his parents are at odds with the beneficiary designation in the testamentary documents executed in the final days of his life, and that this supports a finding of lack of testamentary capacity and undue influence in obtaining the Will. Contestants offer the Payable on Death account beneficiary designations on the Wells Fargo accounts as proof of decedent's true testamentary intent (see Contestants' Will Contest and Grounds of Opposition to Probate of Purported Will at 1:26-28 and 2:5-9; Contestants' Opposition to MSJ/MSA, MPA at 5:11-18; Contestants' Separate Statement of Facts in Support of Opposition to MSJ/MSA at para. 167). [Part 1 of 2]
